biography: We're in business to save our home planet.

latestIgtvVideos.caption:
Grom contests like Ian Walsh’s @ian.walsh's @menehunemayhem are ushering in the next generation of surfers and teaching them the importance of being a good person in addition to being a good surfer. 

Read “Not Just Good Surfers, Good People” through the link in our bio.

The Cheyenne River Sioux once shared a deep bond with Great Plains bison. To revive it, Chris White Eagle, plant manager at Wild Idea Buffalo Company (@wildideabuffalo), brought local youth on a traditional buffalo hunt. This is the story of how one man is reconnecting his community to their past and inspiring a new generation for the future. Watch the story through the link in bio.

As a former climate advisor to the Obama White House and an alpine climber with dreams of big summits, @mollykawahata has dedicated her life to taking on seemingly insurmountable challenges. But it’s her personal struggle with mental health that gives her a profound understanding of how to harness the power of the mind to create change.

"The Scale of Hope" follows Molly as she prepares for an expedition in the Alaska Range while working to create a new climate narrative that centers her favorite question—"What can I do to help?"—with a surprising answer. Find a screening near you through the link in bio.

Film by: Josh Murphy / @joshbonesmurphy, Produced by: Laura Wagner / @liarsandthievesinc, Featuring: Molly Kawahata / @mollykawahata

Welcome to Working Knowledge—a new series of stories grounded in wild places and activism, from around the world and close to home. You'll hear from Elders, young people, mentors and teachers. Each story is an invitation for new voices to rise, for we all have much to teach and much to learn. Read more through the link in bio.

The clothing industry has a plastic problem.

Most synthetic clothing—think polyester or nylon—starts as petroleum that’s been extracted from the earth then distilled to create the plastic-based fibers we wear. About 60 percent of clothing is made from these types of plastics, according to the UN. By 2030, it’s expected to hit 73 percent. And for Big Oil and gas, that’s welcome news. As transportation moves away from fossil fuels, experts say plastic will become a lucrative way for the industry to maintain demand.

Watch “The Monster in Our Closet” through the link in bio to learn more. 

Film directed by Kathryn Francis / @kathrynbays & Nicole Gormley / @gromlet

Plastics are essential to building durable, high-performance clothing. But they’re also accelerating the environmental crisis, from the fossil fuels used to make materials like polyester to the plastic pollution that piles up once that clothing has been tossed. Through the eyes of a lawyer, a climate reporter, and a Patagonia designer, “The Monster in Our Closet” uncovers the dangerous threads that connect the clothing industry to the oil and gas industry and what we can all do on the individual, business, and government levels to create the change that our planet needs.

Watch it through the link in bio.

Film directed by Kathryn Francis / @kathrynbays & Nicole Gormley / @gromlet

The path to enlightenment begins at the world’s deadliest wave. Gerry Lopez (@gerrylopezsurfboards), aka Mr. Pipeline, is one of surfing’s most enigmatic heroes—a Zen Buddhist on land who built his early career on cutthroat, aggressive surfing. Patagonia Films’ “The Yin & Yang of Gerry Lopez”—directed by award-winning filmmaker Stacy Peralta (@peraltastacy) —follows one of the most influential surfers and surfboard shapers of all time as he brings surfing to new frontiers while pursuing stillness of body and mind. 
 
Come ride the lightning and find a screening through the link in bio.

Film by Stacy Peralta / @peraltastacy and produced by Monika McClure / @monikamcclure

Unable to travel overseas due to the ongoing pandemic, Katsutaka "Jumbo" Yokoyama went to the island of Yakushima in Spring 2020 in search of new walls to climb. With its abundance of high-quality rock, he was convinced that Yakushima had the potential to become a world-class climbing destination; but difficulties with local climbers left a bad taste in his mouth. ⁠
⁠
The following year, Jumbo went back to Yakushima with Keita Kurakami (@keitakurakami), determined to make things right and develop the new routes in cooperation with the local climbing community. ⁠
⁠
What the full film from @patagoniajp through the link in bio.

taggedUsers.fullName: 倉上 慶大   Keita Kurakami

latestIgtvVideos.caption:
Jack Cramer (@typetwofunny) and his climbing partners Grant Kleeves (@grantkleeves) and Drew Smith (@_drew_smith_) headed to Alaska for maximum glory, minimal effort. Instead, near-tropical temps and a close call made them urgently reconsider what it means to win or lose when you gamble in the mountains.

Video: Drew Smith / @_drew_smith_, Jack Cramer / @typetwofunny

“The climate crisis we’re facing is an opportunity for people to recognize the power of Indigenous knowledge and to recognize that tribes have the ability, if we could empower them, to help co-manage the environment up and down the state.”—Kouslaa Kessler-Mata, Yak Tityu Tityu Yak Tiłhini Northern Chumash and Yokut
 
Along California’s windswept coast, in the rocky crags and tide pools, California Indians (as this group prefers to be called) have subsisted for millennia on fish, abalone and seaweed, never taking more than can be naturally replaced. The effects of the climate crisis combined with unsustainable harvesting by non-native people have decimated their traditional food sources. Today, despite regulations, they continue the hard work of subsistence gathering and keep their cultural practices alive.
 
Watch Holdfast through the link in bio.

Falconer Shawn Hayes (@shawnhayesfalconry) leads a life of devotion—to both his falcons and to the mountains and prairies beneath their wings. For Shawn, the ancient practice of falconry is more than a deep partnership with raptors: it’s become his life’s work. Through his skill and finesse with these birds of prey he’s carved a space for himself in a culture where people of color haven’t always been welcome. Shawn’s role as an American falconry ambassador has taken him to the far reaches of the globe, through strongholds of tradition and conservation. This film is about more than what humans can train birds to do—it’s about what those birds can teach us about living in partnership with wild creatures and wild places. 

Film by: Brett Marty (@brettmarty) & Josh Izenberg (@onlyhumanfilm) from Speculative Films (@speculativefilms)

Visit the link in bio and find info on how to host a screening.

“In my late 20s I met Yvon Chouinard who later introduced me Doug Tompkins. Doug was the one who came up with the ‘Do Boys.’ He said he ran into the phrase in Japan. It was the title of one of those comic books popular in that country about a group of guys who go around having adventures. And as more friends joined us on more adventures, it seemed like a good name for our posse. ‘We don’t just talk about doing stuff,’ Yvon said, ‘we do it.’” —Rick Ridgeway, Life Lived Wild⁠
⁠
In telling the stories of 26 expeditions to the most remote regions of the world, Rick Ridgeway’s new memoir, Life Lived Wild, chronicles his shift from someone fascinated by wild places to someone dedicated to saving them.⁠
Explore the newest @patagoniabooks through the link in bio.

latestPosts.caption:
We commented about this last Wednesday after the news broke, but we want to be clear on our stance about the recent news of the United States Forest Service, because protecting public lands is core to our purpose: Moving the USFS to Utah will gut the agency. By shutting down its research stations, culling its staff, and moving the headquarters to Salt Lake City, it will be surprising if USFS can effectively manage anything at all.

The only beneficiaries of the move and other rollbacks to public land policy from this past year are billionaires and extractive industries.

Patagonia will continue working with our Tribal and nonprofit partners to protect public lands and advocate for more funding for USFS and the Bureau of Land Management.

Find ways to support public lands at Patagonia Action Works. Link in bio.
latestPosts.alt: Photo by Patagonia on April 06, 2026. May be an image of text that says 'Moving the United States Forest Service headquarters to Utah will gut the agency and sabotage its ability to manage the 193 million acres of forests and grasslands it oversees. The only beneficiaries are billionaires and extractive industries. Find ways to support public lands at Patagonia Action Works.'.

Whatever keeps you climbing, we have the gear to get you there. You in?

latestPosts.caption:
Patagonia Ambassador Clare Gallagher had big plans for her 2025 season. Things ended up a little bit differently. 

Watch the full film at the link in bio.

For a 21-year-old, Jack Ho (@jackfromtown) has had a hell of a career. Born and raised on O‘ahu, Hawai‘i, right next to the world-famous beaches of Waikiki, Jack started surfing when he was 5 years old and he hasn’t left the water since. That passion only intensified when, at age 13, his dad bought him a foil, which he pursued with a dedication that would quickly make him one of the best in the world. Two years later, he and a group of friends discovered downwinders, and the “Voyager Foilers” have spent the past half-decade pushing the limits of what’s possible on a foil … though Jack can still be found off the shore of O‘ahu paddling his short and longboards to catch the next swell.

“My love for surfing started at a young age, and no matter what I do, I can’t shake it off,” he says. “It’s a passion that sticks with me, and the root of it is riding waves every type of way, getting out there and enjoying what my home has to offer. That’s so valuable to me and something I take a lot of pride in.”

So hyped to get on the water with you, Jack. Welcome to the team.

Cash Berzolla (@cash_berzolla) finding a little shade at Peʻahi. 

Photo: Erik Aeder (@erikaederphotography)

latestPosts.caption:
While establishing “La Casa de las Diosas” (5.12?, C1, 220 m)—the first ascent of Mini Asgard, in Baffin Island—Pachi Ibarra (@pachi_ibarra), Violeta Sepúlveda (@violeta.sep), and Céline Jaccard always had one thing clear: the goal of the trip wasn’t a summit or a grade. 

Their objective was always to be at home in that place, accepting whatever the mountains had to offer and enjoying the process—both as a team and as individuals. In Céline’s words: “That space felt feminine, healthy, and beautiful.”

Watch the full story at the link in bio.
Nature is always making a spectacle. Our kids’ and baby clothing is made to get them out there to see it.
